refined_fr.refined_fr: CR-NGHE-005: The NGHE solution shall derive performance requirements from worksite mission context.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates three distinct obligations: (1) deriving requirements from context, (2) recording them, and (3) managing them over time. Derivation is the primary functional obligation in the problem domain; recording and managing are implementation concerns that belong in the DP/IM layers. The refined FR focuses on the core functional need: translating worksite mission inputs into explicit performance targets (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability). This remains solution-neutral and testable against worksite inputs.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verify that given a worksite mission context (e.g., site layout, production targets, safety constraints), the system produces a documented set of performance requirements that are traceable to those inputs and satisfy acceptance criteria (documented, traceable, baselined).
sub_frs: CR-NGHE-005.1: The NGHE solution shall accept worksite mission context inputs (e.g., production targets, site constraints, safety policies).
CR-NGHE-005.2: The NGHE solution shall derive quantitative performance targets (e.g., cycle time, energy efficiency, availability) from worksite mission context.
CR-NGHE-005.3: The NGHE solution shall record derived performance requirements in a retrievable format.
CR-NGHE-005.4: The NGHE solution shall maintain a history of performance requirement baselines and calibration updates.

candidate_dps: DP-NGHE-005.1: Establish a parameterized mission-context capture mechanism that ingests worksite operational inputs (safety plan constraints, mission objectives, environmental conditions) and maps them to a standardized performance requirement template, with version control and audit trail.

DP-NGHE-005.2: Implement a rule-based inference engine that translates worksite mission parameters (hazard classification, operational tempo, personnel density, equipment constraints) into quantified performance targets via a documented decision matrix, with traceability links to source inputs.

DP-NGHE-005.3: Design a hierarchical baseline registry that stores performance target snapshots, calibration deltas, and justification records, enabling rollback and historical comparison of requirement evolution against worksite context changes.

DP-NGHE-005.4: Establish a federated data model where worksite mission context (safety plan, policies, operational constraints) is maintained as a separate authoritative source, with explicit synchronization and change-notification mechanisms to downstream performance requirement derivation.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-NGHE-005.2 + DP-NGHE-005.3 (combined): Implement a rule-based inference engine that translates worksite mission parameters into quantified performance targets via a documented decision matrix with traceability, coupled with a hierarchical baseline registry that maintains performance target snapshots, calibration deltas, and justification records. This combination ensures both derivation logic transparency and historical auditability.

potential_sources: Tight coupling between inference engine (DP-NGHE-005.2) and baseline registry (DP-NGHE-005.3) if decision matrix and storage schema are not cleanly separated by interface.
Dependency on external worksite context source (DP-NGHE-005.4) introduces coupling if change notification is synchronous or blocking.
Risk of circular dependency if performance targets feed back into mission context refinement without clear ownership boundaries.
coupling_check.notes: Coupling is manageable if: (1) decision matrix is versioned and decoupled from inference engine implementation, (2) baseline registry exposes a query/notification API rather than direct state mutation, (3) worksite context ingestion is asynchronous with explicit schema versioning.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Worksite mission context (safety plan, operational policies) is available in machine-readable or semi-structured form; risk if inputs are unstructured narrative documents requiring manual interpretation.

ASSUMPTION: Decision matrix (rules mapping mission parameters to performance targets) can be defined and maintained by domain experts; risk if rule set becomes intractable or contradictory as worksite complexity grows.
ASSUMPTION: Traceability from performance targets back to source mission inputs is achievable via metadata tagging; risk if source inputs are ephemeral or lack unique identifiers.
ASSUMPTION: Calibration updates and baseline changes are infrequent enough to support manual review and approval; risk if operational tempo demands real-time re-derivation.
RISK: No explicit mechanism defined for conflict resolution if mission context inputs contradict each other or existing performance targets.
RISK: History/audit trail may grow unbounded without retention policy, impacting query performance and storage.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR-NGHE-005 to explicitly state: (1) the expected frequency and format of worksite mission context inputs, (2) the approval/validation gate for derived performance targets before deployment, (3) the scope of 'history' (e.g., retain last N baselines, or all changes within T days), and (4) the escalation path if mission context and current performance targets are detected as misaligned. Additionally, introduce a separate FR for 'Change Impact Analysis' to ensure that worksite context updates trigger explicit re-evaluation of affected performance targets rather than silent derivation drift.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Mission planning and performance target derivation layer that owns the rule-based inference engine, decision matrix documentation, and hierarchical baseline registry for worksite mission parameters

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives worksite mission parameters (location, objectives, constraints, environmental conditions) from mission planning or operator input

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Outputs derived performance targets, calibration deltas, and justification records to Vehicle and Teleoperation for execution and monitoring

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety constraints and thresholds; outputs safety-qualified performance targets and baseline snapshots for Safety Services validation

module_containment_scores.notes: Highest cohesion: rule-based inference, decision matrix, and baseline registry are all mission-level abstractions. NGHE Solution is the natural owner of derivation logic and historical auditability. Minor diffusion: performance targets must flow to Vehicle/Teleoperation for execution, but this is expected cross-module communication via Telemetry interface.

Poor fit: Vehicle is a physical/execution platform, not a planning/derivation layer. Placing inference engine and baseline registry here couples mission logic tightly to hardware, violates separation of concerns, and makes reuse across vehicle types difficult. High diffusion: decision matrix and justification records would need to propagate back to mission planning.

Weak fit: Teleoperation is an operator interface and control layer, not a mission derivation layer. Placing inference engine here conflates operator commands with algorithmic target derivation. Baseline registry would be orphaned from mission context. High diffusion: rule-based logic and historical records belong upstream in planning, not in real-time control.

Very poor fit: Safety Services is a constraint/validation layer, not a derivation engine. Placing the inference engine and baseline registry here inverts the dependency: safety should validate targets, not generate them. High diffusion: mission parameters, decision matrix, and performance targets would all need to originate elsewhere.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the natural owner of mission-level derivation logic because it sits at the planning/abstraction layer where worksite mission parameters are first interpreted and translated into quantified targets. The rule-based inference engine and hierarchical baseline registry are mission-planning artifacts, not vehicle execution or operator interface concerns. Containment is strong (0.92) because both the decision matrix and baseline registry are cohesive mission-planning abstractions. Diffusion is minimal (0.08) because the only external flow is the output of derived targets via Telemetry to downstream execution modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation), which is the intended architectural pattern.
containment_risks: Risk: If NGHE Solution is not yet defined as a distinct module in the architecture, this assignment may require creating a new planning/derivation layer. Mitigation: Confirm that NGHE Solution exists as a module boundary; if not, propose its creation as a mission-planning/derivation layer.

Risk: The baseline registry (historical snapshots and calibration deltas) may require persistent storage that is not owned by NGHE Solution. Mitigation: Define a clear interface (e.g., via Telemetry or a new persistence interface) for baseline registry read/write operations to a shared data store.

Risk: Safety Services may need to validate or constrain the inference engine's outputs in real-time. Mitigation: Ensure SafetyAlert interface is bidirectional so Safety Services can provide constraints to the inference engine and receive qualified targets for validation.

Risk: If multiple vehicle types or mission domains are supported, the decision matrix and inference rules may need to be parameterized or modular. Mitigation: Design the inference engine to accept pluggable rule sets and decision matrices, avoiding hard-coded mission-specific logic.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: No revision to DP choice is necessary. The selected DP (combined DP-NGHE-005.2 + DP-NGHE-005.3) is well-suited to the NGHE Solution module boundary. However, ensure that the DP specification explicitly addresses: (1) the interface contract for receiving mission parameters via Tasking, (2) the format and versioning of the decision matrix for traceability, (3) the schema and retention policy for the baseline registry, and (4) the mechanism for SafetyAlert constraints to flow into the inference engine. These details will strengthen the DP-to-IM mapping and reduce implementation ambiguity.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-NGHE-005.2 + DP-NGHE-005.3 (combined) directly addresses FR-NGHE-005 by specifying a rule-based inference engine that translates worksite mission parameters into quantified performance targets, coupled with a hierarchical baseline registry for historical auditability. The DP is solution-agnostic, logically sound, and traceable to the functional requirement. Both acceptance criteria (documented performance targets traceable to worksite inputs; history of baselines/calibration updates) are explicitly addressed by the DP specification.

diffusion_risks: Minor diffusion (0.08): Performance targets must flow to Vehicle and Teleoperation modules for execution. This is expected cross-module communication via the Telemetry interface and does not violate separation of concerns.

Containment risk: Baseline registry persistence layer is not explicitly owned by NGHE Solution. If the registry requires external storage (database, file system), the DP-to-IM mapping must define a clear interface contract for read/write operations to avoid orphaned ownership.

Containment risk: SafetyAlert interface is bidirectional but the DP specification does not explicitly detail how safety constraints flow into the inference engine. The inference engine must be designed to accept safety thresholds as input parameters, not as post-hoc validation only.

Containment risk: Decision matrix and inference rules may become scattered if not centralized as a single, versioned artifact within NGHE Solution. Risk of diffusion if rules are duplicated or embedded in downstream modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation).
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the natural and correct owner of this DP. Containment score of 0.92 is justified: the rule-based inference engine, decision matrix, and baseline registry are all mission-planning abstractions that belong at the derivation layer, not at execution (Vehicle), operator interface (Teleoperation), or validation (Safety Services) layers. The module boundary is well-defined and cohesive. Diffusion is minimal because external flows (Tasking input, Telemetry output, SafetyAlert bidirectional) are intentional architectural patterns, not leakage of core logic.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The IM boundary (NGHE Solution) is clearly scoped and the external interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert) are well-defined. Verification can be performed by: (1) confirming that the rule-based inference engine produces documented performance targets traceable to worksite mission parameters (Tasking input), (2) validating that the baseline registry maintains snapshots and calibration deltas with justification records, (3) tracing outputs via Telemetry to downstream modules, and (4) confirming SafetyAlert constraints are incorporated into target derivation. Acceptance criteria are directly verifiable against the IM boundary.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. The FR is allocated to a single, well-defined IM (NGHE Solution) with explicit ownership of derivation logic and historical auditability. The allocation avoids fragmentation across Vehicle, Teleoperation, or Safety Services. Interfaces are explicit and directional. However, clarity could be strengthened by: (1) explicitly defining the persistence interface for the baseline registry, (2) detailing the schema and versioning of the decision matrix, and (3) specifying the mechanism for SafetyAlert constraint injection into the inference engine.

recommended_revisions: Strengthen DP specification to explicitly define the interface contract for receiving mission parameters via Tasking (schema, format, validation rules).
Add DP detail specifying the decision matrix format, versioning scheme, and traceability mechanism to ensure documented derivation logic is auditable and reproducible.

Define the baseline registry schema, retention policy, and persistence interface (e.g., read/write operations to a shared data store) to clarify ownership of historical records and avoid orphaned storage.

Explicitly specify how SafetyAlert constraints (safety thresholds, operational limits) flow into the inference engine as input parameters, not as post-hoc validation. Ensure the DP describes constraint incorporation logic.
Add DP requirement that the inference engine and decision matrix be parameterizable (pluggable rule sets) to support multiple vehicle types and mission domains without hard-coded logic.
Confirm that NGHE Solution exists as a distinct module boundary in the architecture. If not, propose its creation as a mission-planning/derivation layer with clear ownership and interfaces.

Define acceptance test cases that verify: (1) performance targets are traceable to worksite inputs, (2) baseline registry maintains historical snapshots with calibration deltas, (3) decision matrix is documented and versioned, and (4) safety constraints are incorporated into target derivation.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. The DP is logically sound, the IM boundary is well-defined and appropriately scoped, and verification is feasible. However, the recommended_revisions identify several critical details that must be resolved before full architectural commitment: (1) persistence interface for baseline registry, (2) decision matrix schema and versioning, (3) SafetyAlert constraint injection mechanism, and (4) parameterizable rule sets for multi-vehicle support. These are not showstoppers but represent design decisions that should be decomposed into sub-FRs to ensure they are explicitly addressed and traced. Given high safety criticality, deriving sub-FRs for constraint incorporation and auditability is prudent to prevent downstream integration failures.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-005.1: Derive and document performance targets from worksite mission parameters (Tasking input) using a rule-based inference engine with explicit traceability to source parameters.
FR-NGHE-005.2: Maintain a versioned baseline registry that records historical performance target snapshots, calibration deltas, and justification records for auditability and reproducibility.

FR-NGHE-005.3: Incorporate safety constraints (thresholds, operational limits) from SafetyAlert interface into the inference engine as input parameters to ensure derived targets respect safety boundaries.
FR-NGHE-005.4: Parameterize the decision matrix and inference rules to support multiple vehicle types and mission domains without hard-coded logic, enabling rule-set pluggability.
FR-NGHE-005.5: Define and enforce a persistence interface for the baseline registry (schema, read/write operations, retention policy) to clarify ownership and avoid orphaned storage.

revise.why: The DP specification (DP-NGHE-005.2 + DP-NGHE-005.3) is logically sound but lacks sufficient detail on critical design decisions identified in recommended_revisions. Before proceeding to sub-level decomposition, strengthen the DP to explicitly address: (1) Tasking input schema and validation rules, (2) decision matrix format and versioning scheme, (3) baseline registry persistence interface and retention policy, (4) SafetyAlert constraint injection mechanism (not post-hoc validation), and (5) parameterization strategy for rule sets. These refinements will reduce ambiguity and provide clearer acceptance criteria for sub-FRs. The revisions do not change the core DP logic but make it more precise and implementable.

assumptions: NGHE Solution exists or will be created as a distinct module boundary with clear ownership of mission-planning and derivation logic.
Tasking interface provides structured mission parameters (worksite location, vehicle type, mission objectives, constraints) in a well-defined schema.
SafetyAlert interface is bidirectional and can inject safety thresholds and operational limits into the inference engine at runtime.
Baseline registry persistence is external to NGHE Solution (e.g., shared database or file system) and requires an explicit interface contract.
Decision matrix and inference rules are centralized as a single, versioned artifact within NGHE Solution to prevent diffusion.
Performance targets derived by NGHE Solution are consumed by Vehicle and Teleoperation modules via Telemetry interface without modification.
open_risks: Persistence interface for baseline registry is not explicitly owned; risk of orphaned storage if external database/file system is not contractually assigned.
SafetyAlert constraint injection mechanism is not detailed; risk of safety constraints being applied post-hoc (validation) rather than integrated into inference logic (design-time).
Decision matrix and inference rules may become scattered across modules if not centralized and versioned; risk of rule duplication and inconsistency.
Parameterization strategy for multi-vehicle support is not specified; risk of hard-coded logic that limits extensibility.
Tasking input schema and validation rules are not defined; risk of malformed inputs causing inference engine failures or unpredictable target derivation.
High safety criticality requires explicit traceability of safety constraints through the inference engine; any gap in constraint incorporation could lead to unsafe target derivation.
evidence_needed: Revised DP specification with explicit interface contracts for Tasking input (schema, validation), SafetyAlert constraint injection (mechanism, timing), and baseline registry persistence (read/write operations, retention policy).
Decision matrix format specification (e.g., rule syntax, parameter definitions, versioning scheme) with example rules for at least one vehicle type and mission domain.
Baseline registry schema definition (fields, data types, retention policy) and persistence interface contract (API, storage technology, access control).
Parameterization strategy document describing how inference rules and decision matrix are made pluggable for multiple vehicle types without code changes.

Acceptance test cases for each sub-FR, including: (1) traceability of performance targets to Tasking inputs, (2) historical snapshots and calibration deltas in baseline registry, (3) safety constraint incorporation into target derivation, (4) rule-set pluggability for new vehicle types.
Architecture diagram confirming NGHE Solution module boundary, ownership, and interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert) with explicit data flow and contract definitions.
Safety analysis (FMEA or similar) confirming that constraint injection mechanism prevents unsafe target derivation and that baseline registry maintains auditability for safety-critical decisions.
